annotations.label: Orthogonal matrix

anchor.snippet: real -valued square matrix whose columns and rows are orthonormal vectors

annotations.note: Matrix.orthogonalGroup is the submonoid of square matrices whose transpose is their inverse, equivalent to having orthonormal rows/columns.

annotations.label: Transpose equals inverse characterization

anchor.snippet: a matrix Q is orthogonal if its transpose is equal to its inverse

annotations.note: mem_orthogonalGroup_iff states A is orthogonal iff A * Aᵀ = 1, i.e. the transpose is the inverse.

anchors.snippet: a matrix Q is orthogonal if its transpose is equal to its inverse

annotations.label: Basic properties (invertible, unitary, normal, isometry)

anchor.snippet: An orthogonal matrix Q is necessarily invertible

annotations.note: Invertibility (det a unit, group structure) and unitarity (orthogonalGroup is defined as unitaryGroup) hold, but the normal/isometry characterizations are not bundled as a single statement.

annotations.label: Orthogonal group O(n)

anchor.snippet: The set of n × n orthogonal matrices, under multiplication , forms the group O( n )

annotations.note: Matrix.orthogonalGroup is the group of n×n orthogonal matrices under multiplication.

annotations.label: Special orthogonal group SO(n)

anchor.snippet: The subgroup SO( n ) consisting of orthogonal matrices with determinant +1 is called the special orthogonal group

annotations.note: Matrix.specialOrthogonalGroup is the orthogonal matrices with determinant 1, as confirmed by mem_specialOrthogonalGroup_iff.

annotations.label: Real specialization of unitary, always normal

anchor.snippet: An orthogonal matrix is the real specialization of a unitary matrix , and thus always a normal matrix

annotations.note: orthogonalGroup is literally an abbrev for unitaryGroup over a commutative ring with starRingOfComm, making it the real specialization of the unitary group.

anchors.snippet: An orthogonal matrix is the real specialization of a unitary matrix , and thus always a normal matrix

annotations.label: Orthogonal matrices preserve the dot product

anchor.snippet: Orthogonal matrices preserve the dot product

annotations.note: The correspondence between inner-product-preserving isometries and unitary/orthogonal matrices is formalized, but there is no direct lemma stating an orthogonal matrix preserves the dot product.

anchors.snippet: Orthogonal matrices preserve the dot product

annotations.label: Converse: orthogonal matrices imply orthogonal transformations

anchor.snippet: orthogonal matrices imply orthogonal transformations

annotations.note: A unitary/orthogonal matrix yields a linear equivalence, but it is not packaged as a norm/inner-product-preserving (isometric) transformation.

annotations.label: Identity transformation

anchor.snippet: identity transformation

annotations.note: The identity matrix is the unit element of orthogonalGroup, but there is no dedicated example lemma for it.

annotations.label: Rotation about the origin

anchor.snippet: rotation about the origin

annotations.note: The 2×2 special orthogonal characterization (a=d, b=-c, a²+b²=1) captures planar rotations, but a general rotation-matrix definition is not present.

annotations.label: Reflection across x-axis

anchor.snippet: reflection across x -axis

annotations.note: Reflections in subspaces are formalized as isometry equivalences, but the specific x-axis reflection matrix is not given.

annotations.label: Permutation of coordinate axes

anchor.snippet: permutation of coordinate axes

annotations.note: Permutation matrices are defined and have transpose equal to their inverse (transpose_permMatrix), so they are orthogonal, though this is not stated as membership in orthogonalGroup.

annotations.label: 1×1 orthogonal matrices

anchor.snippet: The simplest orthogonal matrices are the 1 × 1 matrices

annotations.note: No lemma specifically classifies the 1×1 orthogonal matrices as [±1].

annotations.label: 2×2 orthogonality equations

anchor.snippet: The 2 × 2 matrices have the form

annotations.note: The 2×2 orthonormality equations are derived for the determinant-1 (SO(2)) case; the full O(2) entrywise equations are not stated separately.

annotations.label: Rotation and reflection cases of 2×2

anchor.snippet: We can interpret the first case as a rotation by θ

annotations.note: The rotation case of 2×2 orthogonal matrices is captured by the SO(2) characterization; the reflection case and the rotation/reflection dichotomy are not.

annotations.label: 90° reflection is a permutation matrix

anchor.snippet: The special case of the reflection matrix with θ = 90°

annotations.note: No lemma identifies the θ=90° reflection with a specific permutation matrix.

anchors.snippet: The special case of the reflection matrix with θ = 90°

annotations.label: Identity is a permutation matrix

anchor.snippet: The identity is also a permutation matrix.

annotations.note: permMatrix_one shows the permutation matrix of the identity permutation is the identity matrix.

annotations.label: Reflection matrix is symmetric

anchor.snippet: A reflection is its own inverse

annotations.note: Reflections are shown to be involutive/self-inverse (reflection_inv, reflection_involutive) as isometries, but matrix symmetry of a reflection is not stated.

annotations.label: Products of rotation/reflection matrices

anchor.snippet: The product of two rotation matrices is a rotation matrix

annotations.note: Closure of rotations (SO) under products follows from the group structure, but the detailed product rules of rotations and reflections are not stated.

annotations.label: Classification as rotational or not

anchor.snippet: it is always possible to classify orthogonal matrices as purely rotational or not

annotations.note: The determinant-1 condition distinguishing rotational matrices is formalized via specialOrthogonalGroup, but no theorem packages the full rotational/non-rotational classification.

anchors.snippet: it is always possible to classify orthogonal matrices as purely rotational or not

annotations.label: Inversion and rotoinversion

anchor.snippet: represent an inversion through the origin and a rotoinversion

annotations.note: Inversion and rotoinversion matrices are not formalized.

annotations.label: Higher-dimensional rotations need several angles

anchor.snippet: Rotations become more complicated in higher dimensions

annotations.note: The description of higher-dimensional rotations requiring multiple angles is not formalized.

annotations.label: Transposition

anchor.snippet: The most elementary permutation is a transposition

annotations.note: Equiv.swap is the transposition permutation exchanging two elements.

annotations.label: Permutation matrix as product of transpositions

anchor.snippet: Any n × n permutation matrix can be constructed as a product of no more than n − 1 transpositions.

annotations.note: Every permutation is a product of transpositions (swap_induction_on/truncSwapFactors) and permMatrix is multiplicative, but the matrix statement with the n−1 bound is not given.

annotations.label: Householder reflection

anchor.snippet: A Householder reflection is constructed from a non- null vector v

annotations.note: Reflections in a subspace (including hyperplanes) are formalized abstractly, but the explicit Householder matrix I − 2vvᵀ/(vᵀv) is not defined.

anchors.snippet: A Householder reflection is constructed from a non- null vector v

annotations.label: Orthogonal matrix as product of Householder reflections

anchor.snippet: Any orthogonal matrix of size n × n can be constructed as a product of at most n such reflections.

annotations.note: The decomposition of an orthogonal matrix into Householder reflections is not formalized.

annotations.label: Givens rotation

anchor.snippet: A Givens rotation acts on a two-dimensional (planar) subspace

annotations.note: Givens rotations are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rotation matrix as product of Givens rotations

anchor.snippet: Any rotation matrix of size n × n can be constructed as a product of at most

annotations.note: The decomposition of a rotation matrix into Givens rotations is not formalized.

annotations.label: Jacobi rotation

anchor.snippet: A Jacobi rotation has the same form as a Givens rotation

annotations.note: Jacobi rotations are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Orthogonal iff columns form orthonormal basis

anchor.snippet: A real square matrix is orthogonal if and only if its columns form an orthonormal basis

annotations.note: mem_orthogonalGroup_iff' (Aᵀ A = 1) is exactly the orthonormal-columns condition, but it is not phrased as 'columns form an orthonormal basis'.

annotations.label: Determinant is ±1

anchor.snippet: The determinant of any orthogonal matrix is +1 or −1.

annotations.note: det_of_mem_unitary shows the determinant lies in the unitary elements of the base ring, which for ℝ are ±1 (used explicitly in OrthonormalBasis.det_to_matrix_orthonormalBasis_real).

anchors.snippet: The determinant of any orthogonal matrix is +1 or −1.

annotations.label: Determinant ±1 does not imply orthogonality

anchor.snippet: having a determinant of ±1 is no guarantee of orthogonality

annotations.note: No counterexample showing det ±1 does not imply orthogonality is formalized.

anchors.snippet: having a determinant of ±1 is no guarantee of orthogonality

annotations.label: Permutation determinant equals signature

anchor.snippet: With permutation matrices the determinant matches the signature

annotations.note: det_permutation states the determinant of a permutation matrix equals the sign of the permutation.

annotations.label: Diagonalizable over ℂ with eigenvalues of modulus 1

anchor.snippet: an orthogonal matrix can always be diagonalized over the complex numbers

annotations.note: The spectrum (eigenvalues) of a unitary element lies on the unit circle, but complex unitary diagonalizability of orthogonal/normal matrices is not directly formalized.

annotations.label: Orthogonal matrices form a compact Lie group

anchor.snippet: The inverse of every orthogonal matrix is again orthogonal

annotations.note: The group structure (and hence closure under inverse) is formalized, but compactness and the Lie group/manifold structure are not.

annotations.label: SO(n) is a normal subgroup of index 2

anchor.snippet: The orthogonal matrices whose determinant is +1 form a path-connected normal subgroup

annotations.note: SO(n) is defined as the determinant-1 subgroup, but normality, index 2, and path-connectedness are not formalized.

annotations.label: O(n) is a subgroup of O(n+1)

anchor.snippet: thus O( n ) is a subgroup of O( n + 1)

annotations.note: The embedding O(n) ↪ O(n+1) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Orthogonal group is a reflection group

anchor.snippet: an orthogonal group is a reflection group

annotations.note: The fact that the orthogonal group is generated by reflections is not formalized for the matrix orthogonal group.

annotations.label: SO(n) is a subgroup of SO(n+1)

anchor.snippet: SO( n ) is a subgroup of SO( n + 1)

annotations.note: The embedding SO(n) ↪ SO(n+1) is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: SO( n ) is a subgroup of SO( n + 1)

annotations.label: Permutation matrices form the symmetric group

anchor.snippet: Permutation matrices are simpler still; they form, not a Lie group , but only a finite group

annotations.note: permMatrixHom is the monoid homomorphism Perm n →* matrices, but its injectivity/isomorphism onto the permutation matrices is not stated.

annotations.label: Block-diagonal decomposition of orthogonal matrices

anchor.snippet: the effect of any orthogonal matrix separates into independent actions on orthogonal two-dimensional subspaces

annotations.note: The block-diagonal (2×2 block) decomposition of orthogonal matrices is not formalized.

annotations.label: Canonical form of any orthogonal matrix

anchor.snippet: any orthogonal matrix can be brought to the form

annotations.note: The canonical rotation-block form of orthogonal matrices is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: any orthogonal matrix can be brought to the form

annotations.label: Eigenvalues on unit circle; real eigenvalue when n odd

anchor.snippet: If n is odd, there is at least one real eigenvalue, +1 or −1

annotations.note: Eigenvalues lie on the unit circle (spectrum_subset_circle), but the existence of a real eigenvalue when n is odd is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lie algebra consists of skew-symmetric matrices

anchor.snippet: the Lie algebra of an orthogonal matrix group consists of skew-symmetric matrices

annotations.note: so n R is defined as the Lie subalgebra of skew-adjoint matrices, with mem_so giving Aᵀ = -A (skew-symmetric).

annotations.label: Exponential of skew-symmetric is orthogonal

anchor.snippet: the matrix exponential of any skew-symmetric matrix is an orthogonal matrix

annotations.note: exp_mem_unitary_of_mem_skewAdjoint shows the exponential of a skew-adjoint element is unitary; the orthogonal matrix case over ℝ is a special case.

annotations.label: Angular velocity as Lie algebra element

anchor.snippet: the three-dimensional object physics calls angular velocity is a differential rotation

annotations.note: The physics interpretation of angular velocity as a Lie algebra element is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the three-dimensional object physics calls angular velocity is a differential rotation

annotations.label: Exponential gives axis-angle rotation matrix

anchor.snippet: The exponential of this is the orthogonal matrix for rotation around axis v by angle θ

annotations.note: The explicit axis-angle rotation matrix obtained by exponentiation is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: The exponential of this is the orthogonal matrix for rotation around axis v by angle θ

annotations.label: Condition number is 1

anchor.snippet: the condition number is 1 (which is the minimum)

annotations.note: The notion of matrix condition number, and that it equals 1 for orthogonal matrices, is not formalized.

annotations.label: QR decomposition

anchor.snippet: Q orthogonal, R upper triangular

annotations.note: Gram–Schmidt produces an orthonormal basis with an upper-triangular change of basis (the essence of QR), but a matrix QR factorization theorem is not stated.

annotations.label: Singular value decomposition

anchor.snippet: U and V orthogonal, Σ diagonal matrix

annotations.note: Singular values of a linear map are defined, but the UΣVᵀ factorization is not formalized.

annotations.label: Spectral decomposition of symmetric matrix

anchor.snippet: S symmetric , Q orthogonal, Λ diagonal

annotations.note: spectral_theorem diagonalizes a Hermitian matrix as a unitary conjugate of a diagonal eigenvalue matrix; real symmetric matrices are the special case with Q orthogonal.

annotations.label: Polar decomposition

anchor.snippet: S symmetric positive-semidefinite

annotations.note: The polar decomposition of a matrix is not formalized.

annotations.label: Overdetermined system via QR

anchor.snippet: Consider an overdetermined system of linear equations

annotations.note: Solving overdetermined systems via QR is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: Consider an overdetermined system of linear equations

annotations.label: Linear least squares problem

anchor.snippet: The linear least squares problem is to find the x that minimizes

annotations.note: Orthogonal projection onto a subspace gives the norm-minimizing point (the mathematical core of least squares), but the linear-least-squares formulation itself is not stated.

annotations.label: Underdetermined system via SVD pseudoinverse

anchor.snippet: In the case of a linear system which is underdetermined

annotations.note: The SVD pseudoinverse solution of underdetermined systems is not formalized.

annotations.label: Polar decomposition gives nearest orthogonal matrix

anchor.snippet: The polar decomposition factors a matrix into a pair

annotations.note: The polar decomposition and its nearest-orthogonal-matrix property are not formalized.

annotations.label: Averaging algorithm convergence

anchor.snippet: consider a non-orthogonal matrix for which the simple averaging algorithm takes seven steps

annotations.note: The averaging algorithm and its convergence are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: consider a non-orthogonal matrix for which the simple averaging algorithm takes seven steps

annotations.label: Uniform via Haar measure

anchor.snippet: is defined in terms of Haar measure

annotations.note: A Haar/uniform measure on the orthogonal group is not formalized.

annotations.label: QR of normal entries yields uniform orthogonal matrices

anchor.snippet: the QR decomposition of independent normally distributed random entries does

annotations.note: Generating Haar-uniform orthogonal matrices via QR of Gaussian entries is not formalized.

annotations.label: Subgroup algorithm for random orthogonal matrices

anchor.snippet: To generate an ( n + 1) × ( n + 1) orthogonal matrix

annotations.note: The subgroup algorithm for sampling random orthogonal matrices is not formalized.

annotations.label: Nearest orthogonal matrix via SVD

anchor.snippet: The problem of finding the orthogonal matrix Q nearest a given matrix M

annotations.note: The SVD-based nearest-orthogonal-matrix solution is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: The problem of finding the orthogonal matrix Q nearest a given matrix M

annotations.label: Quadratically convergent recurrence

anchor.snippet: a recurrence which converges to an orthogonal matrix quadratically

annotations.note: The quadratically convergent recurrence for the nearest orthogonal matrix is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: a recurrence which converges to an orthogonal matrix quadratically

annotations.label: Stability condition for iterations

anchor.snippet: These iterations are stable provided the condition number of M is less than three.

annotations.note: The stability condition (condition number < 3) for these iterations is not formalized.

annotations.label: SO(n) is not simply connected

anchor.snippet: the +1 component, SO( n ) , is not simply connected

annotations.note: The non-simply-connectedness of SO(n) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Spin(n) is the universal cover of SO(n)

anchor.snippet: Spin( n ) is simply connected and thus the universal covering group for SO( n )

annotations.note: spinGroup is defined in the Clifford algebra, but it is not connected to SO(n) as a universal cover and no covering/simply-connected facts are proved.

annotations.label: Spin(3) = SU(2)

anchor.snippet: the most famous example of a spin group is Spin(3)

annotations.note: The isomorphism Spin(3) ≅ SU(2) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-square: the two conditions differ

anchor.snippet: the conditions Q T Q = I and QQ T = I are not equivalent

annotations.note: The orthogonalGroup is defined only for square matrices, and the non-equivalence of QᵀQ=I and QQᵀ=I for rectangular Q is not formalized.

annotations.label: Orthogonal k-frames and Stiefel manifold

anchor.snippet: matrices with orthonormal columns may be referred to as orthogonal k-frames

annotations.note: Orthogonal k-frames and the Stiefel manifold are not formalized.
